title: Recursive Viability

content.statement: Organize life into nested autonomous units.
content.question: Is each area of your life viable on its own, or does everything need you?
sop: List the sub-systems in your business or life: departments, family roles, personal routines.
For each, rate Autonomy (can it run decisions without you?) and Coordination (does it sync with the rest?), 1-10 each (see calculation, page 4).
Delegate one full routine to the person or system already closest to it, with real decision authority, not just tasks.
Set a lightweight coordination point (weekly sync, shared dashboard) instead of requiring you as the connector.
Test it by stepping back for a week and observing what breaks.
questions: Is each area of your life viable on its own, or does everything need you?
If you had to leave for a month, which part of your team, family, or business would keep making good decisions without checking with you first?
What's one area where you're the single point of coordination for something that could run on its own rules?
content.equation_example: Rate each sub-system 1-10: Autonomy, Coordination. Sub-system 1: Autonomy 7 times Coordination 6 equals 42. Sub-system 2: Autonomy 5 times Coordination 8 equals 40. Sum them for total Viability equals 82. Higher means your systems can each function and still stay in sync without you as the hub; lower means either nothing can run without you, or everything runs but disconnected.
content.closing_statement: Build parts that work alone and still work together.

caption:
Principle Nº 192: Recursive Viability

Organize life into nested autonomous units.

Ask yourself: Is each area of your life viable on its own, or does everything need you?
